title: Iran Ceasefire Collapse Triggers Oil Spike, IMF Cuts Growth Forecast, Trump Orders Trade Embargo on Spain
subhead: Trump declared the US-Iran ceasefire 'over' and launched fresh strikes, sending Brent above $80 and the Dow down 811 points. The IMF cut its 2026 global growth forecast to 3%. In an extraordinary move, Trump ordered a trade cutoff with NATO ally Spain over defense spending disputes.

sections.heading: Iran Ceasefire Collapses as Strikes Resume, Oil Surges Past $80
sections.body:
The fragile US-Iran ceasefire disintegrated on July 8 after Iran launched attacks on at least three commercial tankers near the Strait of Hormuz within 48 hours, including a vessel carrying approximately 8 million cubic feet of liquefied natural gas. President Trump declared the ceasefire 'over' and launched a second night of strikes on Iranian targets, vowing 'retribution.'

Brent crude, the global benchmark, jumped as much as 8% during the session to above $80.50 a barrel, its highest level since the ceasefire was announced last month, before settling around $79.60. The surge was the sharpest two-session oil rally in nearly two months. At least four oil and gas tankers turned back from attempting to transit the Strait of Hormuz, according to ship-tracking data, effectively choking off the waterway that handles 20% of global oil and gas shipments.

Jorge León, head of geopolitical analysis at Rystad Energy, said: 'Tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has essentially stopped, which tells you more about risk perception right now than any statement from Washington or Tehran.' The real test will come after Iran's Supreme Leader Khamenei's burial ceremony later this week, León added, when both sides show whether there is still an appetite for a diplomatic off-ramp.

Stock markets sold off globally. The Dow Jones dropped 811 points, the S&P 500 fell as much as 1.1% before trimming losses, and European indices plunged — Germany's DAX fell 1.6%, Spain's IBEX 35 shed 2.7%, and the FTSE 100 dropped 1.7%. South Korea's KOSPI tumbled 5.3%, also hit by a semiconductor sell-off.
sections.whyItMatters: The ceasefire collapse and tanker attacks re-escalate the single biggest risk to global energy markets. Oil at $80+ is manageable; a sustained Hormuz closure would not be. The market is pricing a heightened probability of prolonged disruption.

sections.heading: IMF Cuts 2026 Global Growth to 3%, Warns Inflation Stalled
sections.body:
The International Monetary Fund on July 8 cut its 2026 global growth forecast to 3.0%, down from the 3.1% projected in April and well below 2025's 3.5% pace. The fund now expects global inflation to reach 4.7% in 2026, up from 4.1% in 2025 — erasing two years of progress against rising prices. Oil prices are projected to be up nearly 32% this year.

The IMF's baseline assumes the Strait of Hormuz reopens later in July and commercial traffic normalizes by March 2027 — assumptions that look increasingly tenuous after the latest escalation. Deputy research director Petya Koeva Brooks said the world economy had 'weathered the shock from the war better than feared,' partly because countries drew on oil stockpiles and non-Gulf producers ramped up output.

The regional divergence is stark. The IMF held its US growth forecast steady at 2.3%, insulated by domestic energy production and AI investment. But eurozone growth was cut to just 0.9%, down from 1.4% in 2025, hit hard by higher energy prices. China is expected to grow 4.6% and India 6.4%, both down from prior years. The fund expects a rebound to 3.4% global growth in 2027.
sections.whyItMatters: The IMF's downgrade — its second this year — formalizes what markets already sensed: the Iran war is not a temporary shock. Even the fund's optimistic reopening scenario leaves growth at a sluggish 3%, and any further escalation would make that look aspirational.

sections.heading: Iran Made Billions Despite Blockade, Sanctions Reinstatement Largely Symbolic
sections.body:
The US revoked the license that had authorized Iranian oil sales under the interim deal, attempting to crush Tehran's revenue. But analysis by Obsidian Risk Advisors shows Iran generated over $23 billion from oil sales in the first half of 2026, exceeding its own pre-war budget forecast of $17.75 billion for the same period.

Before the war, Iran was selling sanctioned oil at roughly $53/barrel. The war created scarcity conditions that pushed prices up, allowing Tehran to sell at $117/barrel in May, even during the US naval blockade, according to Brett Erickson of Obsidian Risk Advisors. Iran had pre-positioned millions of barrels beyond the blockade line via ship-to-ship transfers, and once the MOU lifted the blockade, Tehran moved 55 million barrels out of the Gulf in the first 14 days of the agreement, effectively resetting its storage problem.

Erickson called the US blockade 'counterproductive in many ways,' noting it caused significant damage to Iran's broader economy but failed to curtail oil revenue. Neil Quilliam of Chatham House said Iran 'will not cede its ability to exercise control over Hormuz, as it would risk losing a major point of leverage.'
sections.whyItMatters: The US effort to economically strangle Iran through naval blockade and sanctions has demonstrably failed on the oil revenue front. Iran's ability to sell oil at war-inflated prices — and to evacuate stored barrels during the brief MOU window — means Tehran enters the renewed confrontation financially stronger than before the war began.

sections.heading: Fed Minutes Reveal Deep Split Over Rate Path, AI-Driven Inflation Concerns
sections.body:
Minutes from the Federal Reserve's June 16-17 meeting — the first released under new Chair Kevin Warsh — reveal a deeply divided rate-setting committee. Half of the 18 policymakers who submitted projections supported lifting rates by year-end, while the other half favored keeping them unchanged or cutting. The Fed's key rate currently stands at 3.6%.

'Many' officials said rates would likely need to be higher by year-end, while 'many' also projected they would be unchanged or slightly lower. A few officials believed there was 'a case for raising' rates at the June meeting itself, though they agreed to hold steady in a unanimous vote.

A key concern is the AI investment boom pushing up prices for semiconductors, computing equipment, and electricity. 'Many participants noted that ongoing strong demand for AI infrastructure would likely sustain upward pressure on prices for technology products and electricity,' the minutes stated. Apple recently raised laptop and iPad prices, citing more expensive memory chips.

The New York Fed's measure of consumer inflation expectations rose to 3.7% for the one-year horizon, the highest in nearly three years, while three-year expectations hit a four-year high of 3.3%. Inflation reached a three-year high of 4.2% in May.
sections.whyItMatters: The Fed is caught between two inflation drivers — the Iran war pushing energy prices up, and the AI boom pushing technology and electricity costs higher. With the committee split down the middle and inflation expectations rising, a rate hike before year-end is a real possibility the market has not fully priced in.

sections.heading: Trump Orders Trade Cutoff with NATO Ally Spain
sections.body:
In an extraordinary move at the NATO summit in Ankara, President Trump publicly ordered Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent to 'cut off all trade with Spain, including visits,' calling Spain 'a terrible partner in NATO' and 'a wasted cause.' Trump accused Spain of failing to meet the alliance's new 5% of GDP defense spending target and refusing to let the US use its airspace or bases for the Iran war.

Spain's IBEX 35 index fell 2.7% on the day. The US exported $26.6 billion in goods to Spain last year, running a trade surplus of $5.2 billion. However, Spain's government pointed out that as part of the EU's customs union, individual member states cannot be singled out for trade restrictions — a legal barrier that could render Trump's directive unenforceable in practice.

Trump also threatened broader EU action, saying 'we're going to see what happens with the EU' and accusing the bloc of having 'treated us very badly for years.' UK Prime Minister Starmer warned that household energy bills would be impacted if Middle East tensions continue.
sections.whyItMatters: Whether enforceable or not, the Spain trade threat signals that Trump is willing to weaponize trade policy against NATO allies over defense spending disputes — breaking from the convention that security alliances and trade are separate spheres. It also raises the specter of broader EU-US trade disruption at a moment when transatlantic economic coordination is critical.

sections.heading: UK Bond Market Prices In Rate Hikes as Energy Prices Surge
sections.body:
UK short-dated government bonds suffered their worst day since late March as markets priced in Bank of England rate increases. The yield on two-year gilts rose 15 basis points to 4.35%, with a rate rise in November now fully priced in and a 50% chance of another in December. Just days earlier, markets gave only a 75% chance of one increase by year-end.

The 10-year gilt yield climbed 9 basis points to 4.94%. Eurozone government bond yields also rose, with Germany's 10-year bund yield hitting 3.034%, the highest in almost a month. The inflationary impact of renewed oil price surges is rippling through fixed-income markets, forcing central banks in energy-importing economies to choose between supporting growth and containing price pressures.

The FTSE 100 fell 1.7% to 10,489, its worst day since May, though energy stocks BP (+3.5%) and Shell (+2.2%) bucked the trend. European gas prices surged 5%, with the Dutch TTF benchmark rising over €2.40 to €49/MWh and the UK equivalent up 6p to 116.75p/therm.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK and eurozone are the collateral damage of renewed Middle East tensions. Energy-importing economies face the cruelest dilemma — rate hikes to contain inflation that further choke already-stagnant growth. The eurozone's 0.9% IMF forecast leaves almost no room for policy error.

sections.heading: Asian Economies Face Dual Risk from Energy Shock and AI Valuations
sections.body:
The Asian Development Bank cut its 2026 growth forecast for developing Asia to 4.9% from 5.1%, well below 2025's 5.5% pace, citing the renewed Middle East escalation. But it raised its forecast for Advanced Asia and the Pacific to 2.6%, buoyed by AI-driven demand for electronics and machinery.

South Korea's ADB forecast was raised dramatically to 2.6% from 1.9%, powered by AI-driven semiconductor exports. Singapore reported 38.4% year-on-year export growth in May, driven by AI-related shipments. Yet the KOSPI plunged 5.3% on July 8 in a semiconductor-driven sell-off, illustrating the volatility that accompanies AI concentration.

The ADB warned that 'an abrupt souring of global enthusiasm for AI could turn the tailwind into a headwind,' with stretched tech valuations creating risks of capital outflows and weaker external demand. Inflation in developing Asia is forecast to hit 4.3% in 2026, up from 3.0% in 2025, driven by elevated energy and fertilizer prices. Of 40 developing Asian economies, 24 are forecast to see lower revenues and 24 higher primary expenditure this year.
sections.whyItMatters: Asia's growth story is increasingly bifurcated: AI-linked economies are thriving, but the energy shock hits the region's import-dependent majority hardest. The ADB's warning about AI valuation risk is a reminder that the same tech boom cushioning some economies could become the source of the next shock.

bottomLine: The Iran ceasefire is over, oil is back above $80, and the IMF has formally confirmed what markets already sensed: 2026 global growth will be sluggish at best. But the day's most consequential development may be the revelation that US economic pressure on Iran — the naval blockade and sanctions regime — has fundamentally failed to curtail Tehran's oil revenue, with Iran earning $23 billion in H1 alone by selling at war-inflated prices. The Fed is split down the middle on rates, Trump is threatening trade embargoes against a NATO ally, and UK and eurozone bond markets are pricing in rate hikes that will further squeeze economies already flirting with stagnation. The only bright spots — AI-driven exports in Asia and resilient US growth — come with their own fragility warnings attached.
sources: ABC Australia, AP, BNN Bloomberg, The Guardian, The Telegraph, LA Times/AP, IMF World Economic Outlook Update, ADB Asian Economic Outlook, Obsidian Risk Advisors, Rystad Energy, Federal Reserve Minutes, New York Fed, Chatham House, Dow Jones/Morningstar, Nikkei Asia, Reuters
